topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an irritating persistence, mirroring the dull throb behind Elias’s eyes. He stared blankly at the textbook, the words swimming in an unreadable blur. Every movement felt like wading through thick syrup; lifting his pen required a conscious, deliberate effort. He sighed, a long, drawn-out sound that seemed to vibrate in the quiet study carrel. A flyer, carelessly tossed onto his desk, caught his attention. The bold, stark lettering announced the closure of the humanities department. A strange, heavy quiet settled over him, making the air feel thick and hard to draw in. He blinked slowly, the effort costing him more energy than the realization itself. He just leaned back, the cheap plastic chair groaning in protest under his weight, and let his gaze drift toward the dusty window, watching the afternoon sun move across the quad with agonizing slowness.

[story 2]

"Another coffee," Maya mumbled, her voice sounding rusty even to her own ears. She dragged her feet across the linoleum floor, the simple act of walking feeling like scaling a steep incline. The campus seemed too bright, the vibrant colors of the campus banners grating against her already frayed nerves. She kept rubbing her temples, a familiar pressure building behind her eyes. When the administrative memo finally landed in her inbox, it didn't register as news; it just became another item to be ignored, another piece of paper to push aside. She picked up her lukewarm mug, the ceramic warm against her palms, and took a slow, deliberate sip. The liquid tasted flat, uninteresting. Looking out at the sprawling campus, Maya felt a profound inertia, as if the very gravity of the place was pulling her deeper into a state of suspended animation.

[story 3]

It was the sheer weight of the unmoving afternoon that pressed down on Liam. He sat at his usual spot in the common area, his posture slumped so deeply he was practically melting into the worn upholstery of the sofa. His eyelids felt weighted with lead; opening them to focus on the buzzing activity around him—students laughing, conversations overlapping—was an insurmountable task. He hadn't moved much all morning, just shifting his weight infinitesimally from one hip to the other. When the official announcement finally trickled down, it didn't cause a spike of panic, merely a dull, pervasive sense of inertia. He stared at his reflection in the darkened television screen, seeing a vacant expression that matched the heavy stillness settling over his entire frame. He didn't bother to move, simply letting the quiet seep into his bones.
